Course.title: Course
Course.description: Represents an academic course or subject, used to categorize flashcard sets, tasks, and notes.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Course entity.

name.description: The name of the course or subject (e.g., 'Calculus I', 'World History').

description.description: An optional detailed description of the course or subject.

FlashcardSet.title: FlashcardSet
FlashcardSet.description: A collection of related flashcards, often grouped by topic or course.

id.description: Unique identifier for the FlashcardSet entity.

name.description: The title or name of the flashcard set (e.g., 'Chapter 5 Vocabulary', 'Midterm Review').

description.description: An optional detailed description of the flashcard set.

courseId.description: Reference to the Course this flashcard set belongs to. (Relationship: Course 1:N FlashcardSet)

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the flashcard set was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the flashcard set was last updated.

Flashcard.title: Flashcard
Flashcard.description: An individual flashcard containing a question and an answer, part of a flashcard set.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Flashcard entity.

flashcardSetId.description: Reference to the FlashcardSet this flashcard belongs to. (Relationship: FlashcardSet 1:N Flashcard)

question.description: The question or prompt displayed on the flashcard.

answer.description: The answer or response to the flashcard's question.

lastReviewedAt.description: Timestamp of the last time this flashcard was reviewed.

reviewCount.description: The total number of times this flashcard has been reviewed.

easeFactor.description: A factor indicating the perceived difficulty or ease of remembering the card, used for spaced repetition algorithms (e.g., SM-2 algorithm's E-Factor). Higher values mean easier.

nextReviewAt.description: The calculated timestamp when this flashcard is scheduled for its next review session, based on a spaced repetition algorithm.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the flashcard was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the flashcard was last updated.

definition.description: User-specific collection of academic courses. Each course document is directly owned by the user specified in the path. Includes denormalized 'userId' in path for authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns this course.

params.name: courseId
params.description: The unique identifier for the specific course.

definition.description: Collection of flashcard sets, nested under a specific user's course. Ownership is derived from the parent user and course in the path. Security rules will verify that `resource.data.courseId` matches the `{courseId}` in the path for authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns this flashcard set.

params.name: courseId
params.description: The unique identifier of the course this flashcard set belongs to.

params.name: flashcardSetId
params.description: The unique identifier for the specific flashcard set.

definition.description: Collection of individual flashcards, nested under a specific user's flashcard set. Ownership is derived from the parent user and flashcard set in the path. Security rules will verify that `resource.data.flashcardSetId` matches the `{flashcardSetId}` in the path for authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns this flashcard.

params.name: courseId
params.description: The unique identifier of the course the parent flashcard set belongs to.

params.name: flashcardSetId
params.description: The unique identifier of the flashcard set this flashcard belongs to.

params.name: flashcardId
params.description: The unique identifier for the specific flashcard.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure for UniMate's flashcard feature prioritizes user data isolation, authorization independence, and debuggability. All user-specific data is nested under a top-level `/users/{userId}` collection. This immediately establishes path-based ownership, making security rules simple and efficient by directly comparing `request.auth.uid` with the `{userId}` wildcard in the path.

Specifically, courses are stored under `/users/{userId}/courses/{courseId}`. Flashcard sets, which belong to a course, are further nested under `/users/{userId}/courses/{courseId}/flashcardSets/{flashcardSetId}`. Individual flashcards, belonging to a set, are then nested under `/users/{userId}/courses/{courseId}/flashcardSets/{flashcardSetId}/flashcards/{flashcardId}`.

This structure achieves **Authorization Independence** by denormalizing the `userId` into the path itself. For any document (Course, FlashcardSet, Flashcard), its ownership by `request.auth.uid` is determined purely by the top-level path segment, eliminating the need for expensive and fragile `get()` calls in security rules to check parent ownership. Furthermore, for subcollections like `flashcardSets` and `flashcards`, their immediate parent's ID (`courseId` for `flashcardSets`, `flashcardSetId` for `flashcards`) is included both in the document's field (as per schema) AND in its path. Security rules will enforce that the document's internal `courseId` (or `flashcardSetId`) matches the `{courseId}` (or `{flashcardSetId}`) wildcard in the path. This ensures data integrity and prevents miscategorization without needing `get()` operations for authorization.

**QAPs (Queries are not Filters)** are inherently supported due to the structural segregation. Each collection in this hierarchy (e.g., `/users/{userId}/courses`, `/users/{userId}/courses/{courseId}/flashcardSets`) represents a homogeneous security posture: all documents within it are owned by the `userId` specified in the path. A user can only `list` or `get` documents from paths where `{userId}` matches `request.auth.uid`. Any query attempting to access another user's data will be blocked by rules without requiring complex filtering logic. This ensures that `list` operations are implicitly secured and only return data the requesting user is authorized to see, adhering to the QAP principle.
